Sok. DJPA distinguishes between an adjective and a noun meaning "uncle, father's brother," buying into a folk etymological interpretation (i.e. from ˀḥ ˀb) of the latter going back to Hai Geon. (See the E. S. Rosenthal article cited there, p. 292 no. 26 [so!]) As Rosenthal suggests, this is a highly dubious interpretation. Nor, as Rosenthal discusses, is the "uncle" in question necessarily the father's brother.

This word is often used as a PN in Middle Aramaic monumental texts.

The derived forms of the lemmas given here as ḥdw, ḥdwh, and ḥdwˀ can be identical in consonantal orthography and meanings in most dialects. See DJPA 188a and form section. In JBA and Mandaic, however, the emphatic form can also be he/aḏwā.

entries.end_notes:
The absolute form given here is based on the etymology from ḥdt as suggested by LS and NSG section 26B. The JBA spelling, however, suggests rather a (folk?) etymology from ḥdy.
In Mandaic, the singular hiduta seems to be both "bride" and "groom". In the plural there is a clear distinction: hidutia are "grooms" and hidutata or hidutiniata are "brides".
Perhaps, though, actually from Akkadian ḫidûtu, "joy".

The passage from ThbK2 85:8 cited by LS2 as justification for a Ct stem glossed as "to rejoice" is incorrectly understood. It occurs twice on that page and in both instances is properly understood as Gt of ˀḥd.

entries.form_section: const: ḥēl; pl: normally masc., but often ḥaylÄwÄn when used as an abstract idea or in reference to armies in general or in particular the hosts of Heaven. Note, then that Payne-Smith and Levy created a separate, feminine lemma accordingly, where LS2 has just one. See the citations for an example of the feminine form of the plural used with masculine concord. Cf. also rb ḥyl. "Miracle" is probably a calque from Greek δὐναμις. Also see note s.v. ḥyl adj.
The expression חֵילֵי שְמַיָא renders BH that refers undoubtedly to the astronomical divinities of the ANE, but by the time of the targums surely was understood to refer to angels.

entries.end_notes:
DJPA separates "to forgive" and "to wash" into two verbs. The commonly given etymological connection with ˀḥlˀ "alkali" is dubious.
There are two different semitic roots involved in these multiple lemmata, though, to judge by Arabic and Akkadian: ḥll "to cleanse" and ḫll "to be hollow". Precisely how these two basic forms develop into all the meanings attested across the several Aramaic dialects, and hence the precise number of homonymous roots, remains to be clearly delineated. See also s.v. ḥll vb. #4.

It is quite possible that the C examples from Qumran are not in fact passives: note especially the example from 4QEne1.26.19 where the verb is parallel to a C form of rxq which is also widely used as an active, q.v. The difference from G would then be G="pass beyond" while C="pass by".

entries.end_notes: See also s.v. ḥwlp.

These meanings apparently are denominative of the various ḥlp-based words for different plants whose distinctive feature is an easily peeled skin. Although probably originally (i.e., pre-Semitic) cognate, to judge by Arabic and Akkadian there are two different Semitic roots involved: ḥlp to send forth shoots, and h̗̖lp to change.

Neither ḥmyˁ nor ḥmyr is "yeast" per se as in some other lexica, but rather, as described in the citation from Isho'dadh cited above, leavened dough and/or starter leavening.
The strange but well established Samaritan usage corresponding to standard Hebrew שאר "flesh, relative" would appear to result from a confusion with its consonantal homograph שְּׂאֹר "leaven," which are pronounced the same in the Samaritan tradition.

entries.form_section: also:  <syr>)nxnn </syr>  in the Old Syriac Gospels and other early texts and translations; see note.
entries.end_notes: In the oldest Syriac texts ܐܢܚܢܢ seems to be the unmarked form, while ܚܢܢ is regularly marked for opposition with other pronouns, i.e. "we" as opposed to "you," "he," etc. Cf. verses where both occur, e.g. OS LkCur23:40. (We thus disagree with those who suggest it is merely a literary orthography for the later form, e.g. Nöldeke, Grammar §63, other than perhaps in early poetry where it may clearly be a considered a single syllable. Indeed the olaf is regularly marked with linea occultans in later mss.) Further research is needed to determine when and where this distinction began to weaken, but see the earlier discussion of J. Rödiger in ZDMG 16(1862) 551ff. More recently: Jean-Claude Haelewyck, "The Old Syriac Versions of the Gospels: A Status Quaestionis (From 1842 to the Present Day)", BABELAO 8 (2019), p. 141-179; esp. 162ff. The equivalent form in other dialects is ˀănaḥnā.

The meaning "to deprive of possession" is a calque on Hebrew יוריש.
The unique LJLA usage of the C, Gt and Qt in the sense of "weaning" corresponds to all the other targumic texts that properly use rather חסל where BH has forms of גמל. However the repeated attestation in a single verse makes it difficult to assume a scribal error here. Perhaps rather a whimsical play on words.

The peal generally refers to the subject covering something with itself; the pael to covering with something else.
The meaning differs somewhat from ksy in that the latter refers to simple covering or concealment, whereas this verb almost always refers to an extensive layer of covering.

entries.end_notes: It is clear that there is a peal verb with these consonants meaning "to slay." and using the transitive thematic vowel pattern. Whether this is really a denominative from "sword" rather than simply a transitive variant of ḥrb "to be wasted" is by no means so clear. We assume the latter, but the difference in thematic vowel and, perhaps of greater significance, the difference between the participles ḥreḇ "lying in waste" and ḥrīḇ "slain" show that the distinction was clearly maintained, at least in Syriac. Note, however, the passage cited from P Jos10:28 sub peal meaning #3 in ḥrb #1, where the verb governs both a city and a person as its direct object.

citations.translation: since the 15th of Nisan is Thursday, the first of Iyyar is a Sabbath, and the first of Sivan is a Sunday
